coredata.dc:title: Acid-mediated synthesis coupled with liquid–liquid extraction separation for obtaining red and orange double-color carbon dots: Application for pH, water sensing and cell-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yongqing
Yang, Lijuan
Tian, Meng
Wang, Dandan
Wang, Luchun
Wang, Junji
Quan, Tian
Liu, Shaochi
Zhang, Kailian
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their various advantages, such as low photodamage and high tissue penetration, carbon dots (CDs) with long emission wavelength have gained much attention, and are used to compensate for blue to green fluorescent CDs. However, their synthesis has been challenging. In the present study, an acid-mediated synthesis method was developed for the preparation of long-wavelength CDs by using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as a precursor, phosphoric acid as an acidity regulator. According to the principle of similarity compatibility, liquid–liquid extraction was further adopted to separate red CDs (R-CDs) and orange double-color CDs (O-CDs) from long-wavelength CDs mixture for the first time. By optimizing the factors that affect quantum yield (QY), R-CDs with a QY of 4.16% and O-CDs with a QY of 60.80% were obtained. Structural analysis revealed that both types of CDs had a graphite-like structure with an abundance of various groups on their surface. Their fluorescence colors were quite different because of differences in the nature of these functional groups (e.g. contents of N and P). To explore the application value of two kinds of CDs, the potential use of R-CDs, for pH sensing and O-CDs for water content detection in organic solvents was studied systematically. The results revealed that R-CDs was acid sensitive and can be used to detect different pH values. While O-CDs can be used for the detection of various water content in different organic solvents including ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF and acetone. Finally, due to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the potential of R-CDs as probe for cell imaging was explored preliminarily. Co-localization experiments with commercial dye Lyso-tracker Red showed that R-CDs was localized in lysozyme and can be used for lysosomal imaging, further proving that R-CDs is a potential lysosomal probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acid-mediated synthesis
Liquid-liquid extraction
Dual-colour long wavelength carbon dots
Sensing
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis, liquid–liquid extraction of R-CDs and O-CDs
Investigation of the stability of R-CDs and detection of pH by using R-CDs
Detection of water content in different organic solvents by using O-CDs
Application of R-CDs in cell imaging
The stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium
Preparation and characterization of R-CDs and O-CDs
Study of the optical properties of R-CDs and O-CDs
Fluorescence sensing of pH by using R-CDs
Speculation on the quenching mechanism of the R-CD–pH system
Detection of water content in organic solvents by using O-CDs
Fluorescence responses of O-CDs to water
Speculation on the quenching mechanism of O-CDs to water and the properties of O-CDs in organic solvents
The stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-B. TEM images of R-CDs and O-CDs. C-D. HRTEM images of R-CDs and O-CDs. E-F. FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and O-CDs. G-H. XPS survey spectra of R-CDs and O-CDs.

A. The UV–vis absorption spectra (200–800 nm) of the R-CDs. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (Insets revealed the R-CDs solution under visible light and under UV light of 365 nm).C-I. Photoluminescence emission spectra of O-CDs excited by different wavelength lights in solvents of dichloromethane (C), ethyl acetate (D), ethanol (E), methanol (F), DMSO (G), DMF (H) and acetone (I).J. Fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs at optimum excitation wavelengths in different solvents. K. Images of O-CDs in different solvents under visible light (upper-series) and UV light of 365 nm (lower- series) (Form left to right: dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF and acetone).

A. Fluorescence stability of R-CDs in the presence of various ion concentrations of 1 mM. B. The fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs in the solution with the pH range of 1–7. C. The relationship curves between FL intensity and pH. D. R-CDs solution under visible (lower-series) and 365 nm UV light (upper-series) at different pH conditions (From left to right: pH 1–11).

A-F. Fluorescence spectra of O-CDs for the detection of water in ethyl acetate (A), ethanol (B), methanol (C), DNSO (D), DMF (E) and acetone (F). G-L. Relationships of the relative fluorescence intensity (Fl/Fr) with water content of 0–1% in ethyl acetate (G), 4–50% in ethanol (H), 1–60% in methanol (I), 1–60% in DMSO (J), 5–30% in DMF (K) and 1–10% in acetone (L).

A. Image of melanoma cells in bright field. B. Fluorescence image of melanoma cells incubated with R-CDs for 2 h. C. Image of HepG2 cells in bright field. B. Fluorescence image of HepG2 cells incubated with R-CDs for 2 h.

A. The fluorescence image of HepG2 cells co-incubated with R-CDs for 2 h. B. The fluorescence image of DAPI. C. The combined images corresponding to (A) and (B). D. Fluorescent intensity within the linear region of interest of R-CDs and DAPI. E. The co-localization scatterplot of R-CDs and DAPI.

A. The fluorescence image of HepG2 cells co-incubated with R-CDs for 2 h. B. The fluorescence image of Lyso-Tracker Green. C. The merged images of the corresponding (A) and (B). D. Fluorescent intensity within the linear region of interest of R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green. E. The co-localization scatterplot of R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green.
Preparation, purification processes and application of R-CDs and O-CDs.

head.ce:title: Acid-mediated synthesis coupled with liquid–liquid extraction separation for obtaining red and orange double-color carbon dots: Application for pH, water sensing and cell-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their various advantages, such as low photodamage and high tissue penetration, carbon dots (CDs) with long emission wavelength have gained much attention, and are used to compensate for blue to green fluorescent CDs. However, their synthesis has been challenging. In the present study, an acid-mediated synthesis method was developed for the preparation of long-wavelength CDs by using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as a precursor, phosphoric acid as an acidity regulator. According to the principle of similarity compatibility, liquid–liquid extraction was further adopted to separate red CDs (R-CDs) and orange double-color CDs (O-CDs) from long-wavelength CDs mixture for the first time. By optimizing the factors that affect quantum yield (QY), R-CDs with a QY of 4.16% and O-CDs with a QY of 60.80% were obtained. Structural analysis revealed that both types of CDs had a graphite-like structure with an abundance of various groups on their surface. Their fluorescence colors were quite different because of differences in the nature of these functional groups (e.g. contents of N and P). To explore the application value of two kinds of CDs, the potential use of R-CDs, for pH sensing and O-CDs for water content detection in organic solvents was studied systematically. The results revealed that R-CDs was acid sensitive and can be used to detect different pH values. While O-CDs can be used for the detection of various water content in different organic solvents including ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF and acetone. Finally, due to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the potential of R-CDs as probe for cell imaging was explored preliminarily. Co-localization experiments with commercial dye Lyso-tracker Red showed that R-CDs was localized in lysozyme and can be used for lysosomal imaging, further proving that R-CDs is a potential lysosomal probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one of the most remarkable photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials, have been widely explored because of their valuable properties, such as outstanding optical performance, low biotoxicity, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. These characteristics of CDs broaden their prospects for applications in various aspects of sensing [3], bio-imaging [4], catalysis [5], photodynamic therapy [6], and light emitting diodes [7]. Nevertheless, a large majority of CDs exhibit blue to green fluorescence, which is easy interfered by the autofluorescence of biological tissues in biological imaging, and these CDs have certain photodamage to tissues and cells due to its short wavelength [8,9]. As a result, the use of these CDs for bio-imaging has significant limitations. Recently, owing to their various advantages such as little interference from auto-fluorescence of biological tissues, low photodamage, and high tissue penetration [10,11], CDs with long emission wavelengths (e.g. orange to red light regions) have increasingly been synthesized. However, there is no unified explanation for the formation and fluorescence mechanism of these CDs [12]. Thus, the preparation of long-wavelength CDs remains undeveloped, severely limiting their application in various fields [13,14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore an effective method for obtaining long-wavelength CDs.

It is well known that pH plays a vital role in the polymerisation and carbonisation processes of CDs. Because the pH of the solution affects the form (e.g. protonation) of the functional groups on the CDs, and H+ sometimes shows a catalytic effect, the pH of the solution has a great influence on the fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CDs. For instance, H+ can be used as a catalyst to promote the dehydration reaction, resulting in defects in the CD framework, which in turn leads to a narrowing of the band gap of the CD and a redshift of its fluorescence emission wavelengths [15,16]. Hence, an acid-mediated tuning strategy has been identified as an effective method for the synthesis of CDs with long wavelengths [17,18]. Common measures for obtaining long-wavelength CDs include carbon precursor selection [19], heteroatom doping [20], and surface modification [21]. This strategy may be applicable to many precursors, and the preparation of multicolour CDs can be achieved by changing the pH of the reaction system or adding different kinds of acids [17]. Moreover, when an acid is introduced, some elements in the acid, such as phosphorus, sulphur, and nitrogen, can be doped into the structure of the as-prepared CDs, and the results of this doping can also affect the fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs. Feng et al. proposed that acid-mediated changes in fluorescence behaviour can be achieved by simply changing the pH of the precursor solution [22]. In our previous study, multicolour CDs from blue to orange were successfully obtained by regulating the amount of sulfuric acid with crystal violet as the precursor [23]. However, the types of precursors used in tuning by the acid-reagent engineering strategy are still limited, and corresponding studies using other precursors are needed to verify the effectiveness of this strategy.

On the other hand, because many CDs are synthesized in a larger mixture system. The mixture may contain CDs of different colors, other impurities, small fluorescent molecules, etc. Among them, most impurities can be removed by centrifugation and small fluorescent molecules can be removed by dialysis. While, a process of separation is often required to distinguish CDs of different colors[24]. Silica column chromatography is a commonly used method for the separation of multi-colour CDs. Although it is effective, it has many obvious disadvantages. The separation process is cumbersome and time-consuming, and only a few CDs can be separated simultaneously [25]. Particularly, in the tedious separation process, CDs with small amounts may even be lost owing to the repeated separation processes. Therefore, the ability to synthesize CDs in combination with a facile separation process and easy fluorescence tuning is very desirable. Interestingly, according to the principle of similar-phase dissolution, liquid–liquid extraction may be an approach with potential for not only separating CDs with other impurities, but also separating CDs with different colours. The main reason is that, because the precursors, over-carbonized substances, and CDs with different colours may have different polarities and solubilities, a suitable extraction solvent can separate the CDs from other substances or separate CDs with different colours. In addition, in comparison with silica column chromatography, liquid–liquid extraction has many outstanding advantages, including short extraction time, simple operation process, and low solvent consumption. In our previous study, we successfully used this method to separate CDs from other impurities, and the QY of the CDs was effectively improved [26]. However, no study has used this method to separate CDs of different colors. Therefore, after obtaining the CDs solution using the acid-reagent-engineering strategy, liquid–liquid extraction was further used to separate the CDs with different colours.

In this study, an acid-mediated synthesis coupled with a liquid–liquid extraction separation strategy was proposed for the first time for obtaining double-colour CDs with long emission wavelengths. Using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as the precursor, phosphoric acid as the acidity regulator, and water as the solvent, CDs with red fluorescence (R-CDs) and double-emission CDs with orange fluorescence (O-CDs) were obtained by a facile one-step hydrothermal method, followed by liquid–liquid extraction using dichloromethane as the extraction medium. To obtain CDs with a high QY, the factors influencing QY, including the amount of phosphoric acid, the reaction temperature and reaction time were investigated systematically. Different characterization techniques were employed to analyze the characteristics of R-CDs and O-CDs. Finally, to assess the potential usefulness of these new materials, the applications of R-CDs for pH sensing (direct detection and visual observation) and cell imaging, and the application of O-CDs for water content detection in organic solvents were investigated.

N-Phenyl-o-phenylenediamine (purity > 98 %) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. ltd. DAPI was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co. ltd.Lyso-Tracker Green and DAPI were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co. ltd. Rhodamine B (>99 %) was gained from Adamas Reagent Co. ltd. Melanoma cells and HepG2 cells were purchased from the Cell Center of the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, >98 %) trypsin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco. Penicillin–streptomycin was purchased from Beijing Ding guo biotechnology co. ltd. Phosphoric acid, organic solvents and ions reagents were acquired from Chengdu kelong Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. All the reagents in the experiments were of analytical grade.

Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves were heated in an electric blast drying oven (Yamato DKN412C, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were measured on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). The UV absorption spectra were obtained by using an UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were gained using a JEOL 2100F (JEOL) transmission electron microscopy. The FT-IR spectra were acquired by an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The elemental compositions of O-CDs and R-CDs were analyzed through a K-Alpha + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo fisher Scientific). Fluorescence images of cells were obtained through an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX-73, Olympus) and MTT measurement was carried out on a microplate reader (synergy H1, Biotek).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, liquid–liquid extraction of R-CDs and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
A simple one-step hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize the CDs using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as the precursor, phosphoric acid as the acidity regulator, and water as the solvent. The procedure was as follows. N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine (0.4 g, 0.5 mmol), phosphoric acid (1.25 M, 8 mL), and water (32 mL) were mixed and ultrasonicated for approximately 30 min. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined 50 mL stainless-steel autoclave, and the reaction was conducted at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the blue solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 30 min to remove impurities, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. To separate and purify the CDs, the blue solution was subjected to liquid–liquid extraction as follows. First, the blue solution was transferred to a separatory funnel, dichloromethane with five times the volume of the blue solution was added, and the mixture was shaken and allowed to stand for approximately 20 min. The dichloromethane and water layers were separated and the water layer was extracted three more times with dichloromethane, using the same process, until the dichloromethane layer remained the same. The combined dichloromethane layers were then concentrated by rotary evaporation until the volume no longer changed. The concentrated solution contained the O-CDs. The water layer was dialyzed for 24 h to efficiently remove small molecules (using a dialysis bag with a molecular-weight cutoff of 1000 Da), and the R-CDs were thereby obtained. The two types of CDs were dried to obtain solid powders for further use. The details of the preparation process of the R-CDs and O-CDs are described in Scheme 1
A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the stability of R-CDs and detection of pH by using R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the future application of R-CDs, the stability of the R-CDs under various conditions were determined. For ionic strength: the stability of R-CDs in various NaCl concentrations was determined. 0.3 mL (100 μg·mL−1) of R-CDs was added into 2.7 mL of different concentrations of NaCl solution and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was recorded (Ex = 580 nm). For temperature stability: The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs solution which treated at different temperatures (10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 and 55℃) for 10 min was recorded. For metal ion stability: A solution (300 μL, 1 mM) of the appropriate ion (including Cu2+, K+, Bi3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Eu3+, Ag+, Er3+, Cu2+, Zr4+, Cr4+, Fe2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn4+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+) was added to 2.7 mL of an aqueous solution of R-CDs (100 μg·mL−1) and incubated for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs was recorded, using the same conditions in the presence of the different ions. Finally, the photobleaching resistance of R-CDs solution was studied. In short, fluorescence intensity of R-CDs solution was recorded at different times (0, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 90, 120, 150 and 180 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in different organic solvents by using O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1 mg of O-CDs was added to 50 mL of the organic solvent (Ethyl acetate, methanol, ethanol, acetone, DMSO, or DMF) to form a series of O-CDs stock solutions. Different amounts of water were separately added to the different O-CDs stock solutions, and fluorescence emission was recorded at excitation wavelengths of 490 nm and 510 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of R-CDs in cell imaging
The stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium
ce:section.ce:para: Before doing the cell experiment, the stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium was firstly studied. 0.3 mL of R-CDs (100 μg·mL−1, diluted with PBS) solution was added to 2.7 mL complete culture medium (100 mL DMEM + 10 mL FBS + 1 mL penicillin–streptomycin) with pH value adjusted to 5 (Since R-CDs fluoresced under acidic conditions, the pH was adjusted to 5). Fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded at 580 nm excitation wavelength.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of R-CDs, MTT assays were performed on melanoma and HepG2 cells. First, the melanoma cells or HepG2 cells were added to 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃ in a condition of 5 % CO2. After that, R-CDs at different concentrations (10–1000 μg·mL−1) were separately added to the wells and incubated for another day. Thereafter, 10 μL of MTT (5 mg⋅mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. All the solutions were then removed from the wells, and 150 μL of DMSO in each well was used to dissolve the formazan complex. After shaking the plate for 10 min, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

The melanoma cells or HepG2 cells cultured in DMEM (containing 10 % FBS) were placed into six-well plates and incubated in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. R-CD (100 μg·mL−1) was then added to the plates and incubated with the cells for 2 h, after which the DMEM was removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Images were obtained using an inverted fluorescence microscope. In cell localization experiment, HepG2 cells were incubated with R-CDs (100 μg·mL−1) for 2 h. The cells were then washed three times with PBS, and incubated for 5 min in the dark after adding DAPI (0.03 mM). After careful cleaning three times with PBS, fluorescence imaging was performed using an IX-73 inverted fluorescence microscope (BNA, GFP and RFP fluorescent modules). These applications of the R-CDs and O-CDs are shown in Scheme 1 B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of R-CDs and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine can be used to prepare orange fluorescence-emitting CDs because the electron-donating ability of the N atom can be increased by the phenyl group [9]. However, its potential for the synthesis of red fluorescence-emitting CDs has not yet been explored. Moreover, the separation process of the reported orange fluorescence-emitting CDs was complicated, as it required silica column chromatography [9]. In the present study, a simpler method, acid-mediated synthesis coupled with a liquid–liquid extraction separation strategy, has been developed to obtain two kinds of N, P-co-doped CDs (R-CDs and O-CDs), using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as the precursor. To obtain long-wavelength CDs with a high QY, the factors affecting the QY of CDs were optimized by using the QY of R-CDs as the index. As can be seen in Table S1 , by using 2 mL of phosphoric acid as an acidity regulator, a reaction temperature of 200 °C, and a reaction time of 10 h, the highest QY of R-CDs (4.16 %) was obtained. Under these conditions, the QY of the corresponding O-CDs was calculated to be 60.80 %.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to examine the morphological features of the R-CDs and O-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1
A and 1B, the TEM images show that the R-CDs and O-CDs had relatively uniform spherical structures. Their particle size distributions ranged, respectively, from 2.00 nm to 4.50 nm and from 2.00 nm to 5.00 nm, with average sizes of approximately 3.13 nm and 3.45 nm ( Figure S1 , A and B). Moreover, the HRTEM images in Fig. 1, C and D, reveal that the R-CDs and O-CDs displayed lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.22 nm and 0.23 nm, respectively. These lattice spaces were in keeping with the graphite (100) plane, revealing the graphite-like structure of the O-CDs and R-CDs [27].

Fourier transform infra-red (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted to determine the functional group properties of R-CDs and O-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 E, the broad absorption band between 3410 cm−1 and 2803 cm−1 reveals that OH/NH, carboxylic OH, and CH may exist on the surface of R-CDs [28]. The absorption peaks at 1651 cm−1, 1525 cm−1 and 1467 cm−1 are characteristic of CO, CN/CC, and amide III CN [28,29]. The bands around 1311 cm−1 and 1168 cm−1 represent CN = and COC stretching vibrations, respectively [9]. In addition, the characteristic peaks located at 530, 758, 869, and 1053 cm−1 were attributed to PO4 3−, POH, POC and PO, respectively [30–32]. All the above results illustrated that various chemical groups (such as –NH and –COOH) existed on the surface of the R-CDs, and P doping was also present in the R-CDs. In addition, the absorption bands around 3452 cm−1 and 3367 cm−1, which were attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations, were present in the FT-IR spectrum of the O-CDs (Fig. 1 F). The absorption peaks at 1604, 1496, 1305, and 1143 cm−1 were characteristic of CO/CN, CC, CN=, and COC, respectively. As with the R-CDs, PO4 3−, POH, POC, and PO also existed in the O-CDs. These results revealed the successful doping of N and P into the O-CDs, and the presence of many functional groups such as OH and NH2 on the surface of the O-CDs. As previously reported, surface defect states were the main factor affecting the fluorescence of CDs, and the surface defect states of CDs were influenced by the types of functional groups in the CD structure. As the conjugation length and amount of surface –COOH increased, the band gap decreased and the emission wavelength redshifted as a result of the delocalisation of π electrons [33]. From the FT-IR results above, it can be clearly seen that the surface of the R-CDs had –COOH groups, while the O-CDs had almost none, and this difference may be responsible for the difference in the fluorescence performances of the two types of CDs.

XPS analysis was carried out to further explore the surface groups and elemental compositions of the R-CDs and O-CDs. Fig. 1 G showed that the R-CDs contained C (1 s), N (1 s), O (1 s) and P (2p), in the proportions of 54.06 %, 7.46 %, 31.07 % and 7.41 %, with peak positions at 284.8, 400.19, 531.49 and 133.58 eV, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S2
A) showed five significant peaks for CC/CC, C–PO, CN/COC, C–OH, and CO/CN bonds at 284.3, 284.9, 285.5, 286.1 and 287.3 eV, respectively [9,34]. In addition, the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2 B) had three peaks, at 399.55, 400.55 and 401.4 eV, which can be attributed to amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively, indicating the successful incorporation of N atoms into or onto the carbon framework of the R-CD. The high-resolution O1s peak (Fig. S2 C) can be deconvoluted into four main peaks, at 530.68, 531.4, 532.22 and 533.0 eV, corresponding to OC, PO, CO and OC–OH [28,35]. The P2p spectrum (Fig. S2 D) displayed two peaks at 133.2 and 134.1 eV typical of PO and PC/PN [36]. All of these results were in agreement with the FT-IR results. The XPS spectrum of the O-CDs (Fig. 1 H) revealed that the O-CDs were also composed of C (1 s), N (1 s), O (1 s), and P (2p). The contents of them were separately determined as 66.76 %, 1.90 %, 30.76 % and 0.59 %. Compared with the XPS spectrum of the R-CDs, there were significant differences in the contents of various elements, especially N and P. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S2 E) showed three significant peaks for CC (284.42 eV), CC/CP/CN/COC (284.98 eV), and C–OH (286.1 eV). Three peaks that were assigned to amino N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), and graphitic N (401.3 eV) were present in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2 F) [37]. The high-resolution O1s peak (Fig. S2 G) can resolved into two peaks, at 531.9 and 532.6 eV, attributed to PO and CO [9]. The different bonding nature of O in the two CDs clearly showed that –COOH existed only in the R-CDs, which further verified the FT-IR results. In addition, it can be concluded from the XPS results that the difference between N and P may also influence the difference in fluorescence colour between the two CDs. Previous reports have shown that doping with N is an effective way to prepare CDs with high QYs and long wavelengths, while doping with P can generate more isolated sp2-carbon clusters and defect sites, which can also be used to prepare CDs with long emission wavelengths and enhance their fluorescence [38,39]. The much higher contents of N and P in R-CDs compared with O-CDs may result in a redshift of the fluorescence [33].

In general, it can be inferred that the significant difference in the fluorescence behaviour of the two CDs was due to the different compositions of their surface functional groups and the different functional states of the elements in them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the optical properties of R-CDs and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence and UV–vis properties of the R-CDs and O-CDs were explored. For R-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 A) showed absorption peaks centered at 276 nm–290 nm, which were ascribed to the π–π* transitions of CC and the n–π* transitions of conjugated CO and CN bonds in the core of the R-CDs [40]. There was also an absorption band around 600 nm, with a maximum at approximately 628 nm. This absorption peak led directly to strong red fluorescence emission (600–750 nm), which was due to the local recombination of surface energy and excited state energy [41]. Thus, the appearance of an absorption peak at ca. 600 nm was important for red fluorescence emission. The fluorescence emission spectra of the R-CDs recorded under different excitation wavelengths in aqueous solution were shown in Fig. 2 B. The fluorescence emission remained unshifted despite the change in excitation, and this may be a consequence of the uniform surface states and narrow size distribution of the R-CDs. Moreover, the maximum fluorescence emission from the R-CDs was observed at λex/λem = 580 nm/648 nm. The R-CD solution appeared blue in sunlight and showed strong red fluorescence under UV light (inset of Fig. 2 B).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the O-CDs were also recorded under different excitations in various organic solutions (Fig. 2, C–I), and it can be clearly seen that the O-CDs had double emission. Moreover, as in the case of the R-CDs, the fluorescence emission of the O-CDs remained unshifted regardless of the excitation wavelength. As can be seen in Fig. 2, C–I and Table S2 , in the selected organic solvents, the maximum emission wavelength and fluorescence colour of the O-CDs were different in different solvents, indicating that the fluorescence of O-CDs was affected by the polarity of the solvent: the greater the polarity, the greater was the redshift of the emission wavelength (Fig. 2 J). In addition, the O-CD solution appeared light pink under sunlight (upper series), whereas the colour varied from orange to red under UV light (lower series) (Fig. 2 K).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of pH by using R-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
The stability of the CDs greatly influences the fluorescence detection of the target analytes. Thus, the stability of the R-CDs was explored first. Different concentration of NaCl from 0.1 to 1 M were applied to explore the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs, and the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs were recorded. As exhibited in Fig. S3
A, the fluorescence intensity R-CDs did not change within the NaCl concentration range selected, revealing that NaCl had almost no influence on the fluorescence intensity and R-CDs owned salt tolerance property and desirable properties for further sensing application. Further, the time and temperature stabilities of R-CDs were studied. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs kept stable in the selected time (within 180 min), demonstrating the good time stability (Fig. S3 B). The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs kept stable with 30 ℃. After 30 ℃, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs gradually decreased, but the decline trend was still slow, indicating that the fluorescence of R-CDs still had a certain temperature tolerance (Fig. S3 C). Besides, due to the interference of some metal ions in water system, the stability experiment of R-CDs on metal ions was also done. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A and Fig. S3 D, metal ions had little impact on the fluorescence properties of the R-CDs, demonstrating that the stability of the R-CDs was good.

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs were recorded in the pH range 1–7, and the R-CDs exhibited strong pH-dependent fluorescence behaviour. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs was very strong in the pH range 1–2, but steadily decreased as the pH increased from 3 to 7, and there was little fluorescence in the pH range above 7. Moreover, a good linear relationship was found between the fluorescence intensity and pH values from 1 to 5 (Fig. 3 C). These phenomena demonstrate that R-CDs can be used for the sensing of pH in aqueous solutions in the pH range 1–5, a situation that may be related to the preparation of R-CDs under acidic conditions. It is interesting that, as shown in Fig. S3 E, the fluorescence of the solution could be restored by changing the pH from 7 to 1 with HCl. After changing the pH from 7 to 1 and then back to 7 five times, the fluorescence intensity was hardly changed from the original value under the same conditions, revealing that R-CDs possessed outstanding reversibility and that the pH value might be used for the detection of extreme acids [42]. More exciting, not only can the linear change of fluorescence intensity with pH be observed, but also the colour change of the solution under UV light at different pH values can be clearly seen (Fig. 3 D). The colour of the solutions between pH 1 and pH 11 changed from blue to pink, which could be distinguished by the naked eye (Video A_B). These phenomena are potentially useful for the realization of a dual-mode pH sensor [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Speculation on the quenching mechanism of the R-CD–pH system
ce:section.ce:para: It can be easily seen from the above results that the R-CDs showed an interesting characteristic: the more acidic the environment, the stronger the fluorescence. With increasing pH, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs decreased or even disappeared. According to previous research on CDs of this type, the fluorescence of a CD depends mainly on the surface defect state caused by the surface functional groups of the CD, andthe pH of the solvent often affects the fluorescence properties of the CD by changing the energy levels of the surface states [43]. Interestingly, the R-CDs exhibited excitation-emission independence. As can be seen from Fig. 3 B, with an increase in pH, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs gradually decreased and eventually disappeared, but the fluorescence position did not display a red shift or blue shift. Combined with the UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs at pH 1–8 (Fig. S3 F), it can be seen that none of the absorption peaks of the R-CDs moved or disappeared when the pH was less than 7, whereas the absorption peak at 600 nm disappeared when the pH was>7. Therefore, it can be concluded that fluorescence quenching was caused by significant changes in the surface properties of the R-CDs. Moreover, according to the FT-IR and XPS results above (Fig. 1 E, 1G, and S2, A–D), it can be easily seen that the surface of the R-CDs consisted of various groups such as –OH and –COOH. These functional groups would have different states (protonation or deprotonation) under different pH conditions. More importantly, the number and type of groups would affect the state of the entire R-CDs. When the pH of the solution changed, the protonation or deprotonation of –COOH and carboxylate groups on the surface of R-CDs, and the balance between the phenolic hydroxyl group and phenolate, would affect the fluorescence characteristics of R-CDs [44]. Therefore, it is reasonable to explain the pH-dependent fluorescence characteristics of R-CDs as protonation or deprotonation occurring in the R-CDs under different pH conditions, resulting in differences in the surface properties and fluorescence of R-CDs in solutions of different pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents by using O-CDs
Fluorescence responses of O-CDs to water
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the O-CDs were explored using fluorescence spectroscopy. The O-CDs exhibited outstanding solubility in various organic solvents with different polarities, including dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, acetone, DMSO, and DMF. Moreover, O-CDs showed a red–orange double emission fluorescence in organic solvents, and water can affect the fluorescence properties of O-CDs in various solvents. It can be seen from Fig. 4 , A–F, that with the gradual addition of water, the orange and red fluorescence intensities of the O-CDs decreased. However, the orange fluorescence and red fluorescence intensities decreased differently with the increase in water content in the organic solvent, and the observed colour of the solution therefore changed with the increase in water content ( Figure S4 , showing ethanol as representative). Therefore, O-CDs with dual emission characteristics can be used to detect the presence of water in various organic solvents. The linear response ranges for ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF, and acetone were calculated as 0.1–1 %, 4–50 %, 1–60 %, 1–60 %, 5–30 % and 1–10 %, respectively. The linear regression equations of the O-CDs in these solvents were as follows: y = –0.2676x + 3.0623 (R2 = 0.9905, ethyl acetate); y = –0.0081x + 3.8163 (R2 = 0.9948, ethanol); y = –0.0157x + 3.6566 (R2 = 0.9880, methanol); y = –0.0188x + 2.9620 (R2 = 0.9813, DMSO); y = –0.0206x + 3.2049 (R2 = 0.9966, DMF); and y = –0.0288x + 3.2385 (R2 = 0.9925, acetone) (Fig. 4, G–L), where y represented Fl/Fr, and × was the amount of water in the organic solvent. The limits of detections (LODs) in ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF, and acetone were calculated as 0.04, 1.67, 1.33, 0.33, 1.67, and 0.33 %, respectively. Notably, because water is insoluble in dichloromethane, O-CDs were unsuitable for the detection of water in dichloromethane. Overall, O-CDs can be employed as sensors to detect the water content in organic solvents, and thus they had the potential to determine the purity of various organic solvents. In comparison with other studies on the use of CDs for water detection in organic solvents ( Table S3 ), the O-CDs showed satisfactory LODs, and the range of applicable organic solvents was wide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Speculation on the quenching mechanism of O-CDs to water and the properties of O-CDs in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4, A–F, the water content can be measured by the change in fluorescence intensity, even at low water content. With increasing water content, the fluorescence gradually weakened, and the emission wavelength gradually redshifted ( Figure S5 , A–F). The reason for this fluorescence quenching can be understood as follows. In the liquid–liquid extraction separation process, the O-CDs were obtained in the dichloromethane layers; thus, they had little or no solubility in water. In the process of increasing the water content in organic solvents, the O-CDs gradually aggregated because their poor dispersion in water led to excessive resonance energy transfer and π-π interactions, resulting in fluorescence quenching [45]. Furthermore, swelling and shrinking are typical phenomena in graphene-like nanomaterials. Water can cause swelling, which also leads to fluorescence quenching of the O-CDs. In addition, water molecules can exist as hydronium ions and act as electron-sucking compounds, which would also lead to the fluorescence quenching of O-CDs [46]. Thus, under multiple interactions, the fluorescence of the O-CDs was quenched by the addition of water. The reason for the redshift of the emission wavelength after adding water gradually can be explained as follows: as a result of the hydrogen bonding in water, the conjugation length of some surface groups increased, the band gap caused by π-electron delocalisation decreased, and hence the emission wavelength redshifted [47].

In addition, it can be obviously seen from Figure S6 that with the increase of the solvent polarity, the maximum emission peak shifted gradually from 561 to 577 nm at a single excitation wavelength. Obviously, the shift of the emission spectrum of the O-CDs was dependent on solvent polarity [48]. Besides, as reported, the surface state should be primarily responsible for the excitation-independent fluorescence properties [49]. The O-CDs here showed solvents excitation-independent emission behavior in all solvents (
Figure S7 ), suggesting that their emission behaviors were also strongly related to the surface state of O-CDs. O-CDs may have different surface states in different solvents, so they may have different fluorescence behaviors. For example, methanol and ethanol belonged to the protonated solvents, and their fluorescence spectra were very close. The reason for this phenomenon was the formation of hydrogen bonds between the functional groups on the surface of O-CDs and alcohol molecules (e.g. oxygen and nitrogen atoms). Moreover, in different solvents, O-CDs had different sensitivity to water, which may also be related to the polarity of the solvent. The greater the polarity, the wider the linear range. Conversely, the smaller the solvent polarity, the narrower the linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium
ce:section.ce:para: Before doing the cell imaging, the stability of R-CDs in cell culture medium was measured. As shown in Figure S8 , with the increase of time, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs in cell culture medium gradually decreased. However, it can be easily seen that within 10 h, the decline had been slow, about 20 %. This decline had little effect on subsequent experiments.

Owing to the various advantages of R-CDs, such as low photodamage, and high tissue penetration, they can be used for cell imaging. Moreover, any fluorescent probe should possess not only optical advantages but also low cytotoxicity. Thus, before evaluating the imaging performance of the R-CDs, their cytotoxicity was investigated, using the MTT assay on different cell lines. As shown in Figure S9 , A–B, in the range of 10–1000 μg⋅mL−1, R-CDs showed no significant cytotoxicity to melanoma and HepG2 cells, indicating that the cytotoxicity of R-CDs was low and that R-CDs could be employed for cell imaging.

Because of the low toxicity and good long-wavelength emission characteristics, R-CDs have been employed for cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 5 , the properties of R-CDs ensured that after adding R-CDs into the six-well plates of the two kinds of cells (melanoma cells, Fig. 5 , A and B, and HepG2 cells, Fig. 5 , C and D), the cells can be imaged in a relatively short time (2 h). Moreover,because of the low toxicity of R-CDs, the imaging time can be extended to 72 h, after which the emission of the R-CDs gradually decreased. These results showed that R-CDs can be used for long-time imaging and can be modified later for the time-consuming detection of certain target sites.

To investigate the exact location of the image of the R-CDs after cells had been co-incubated with an R-CDs, HepG2 cells were separately co-incubated with DAPI and Lyso-Tracker Green (a commercial lysosome-targeted probe). As shown in Fig. 6 , A–D, the red fluorescence of R-CDs almost not covered with the blue one of DAPI, the fluorescent intensity of the linear regions of interest across live cells incubated with R-CDs and DAPI had a big difference. Co-location data was further processed by Image J. It can be seen that there was basically no co-location between R-CDs and DAPI, and the Person correlation coefficient was 0.22 (Fig. 6 E). These results indicated that R-CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm, and can therefore be used for cytoplasmic imaging. Besides, the image of HepG2 cells co-incubated with R-CDs in Red channel can overlap well with the image of Lyso-Tracker Green, the fluorescent intensity of the linear regions of interest across live cells incubated with R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green changed in similar trends (Fig. 7
, A-D). Co-location data was processed by Image J, and mostly overlap between R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green can be seen (Person correlation coefficient was 0.86) (Fig. 7 E). The common location scatter plot further indicated that the imaging site of R-CDs was in lysosomes [9,50]. These all results indicated that R-CDs had ability to enter into lysosomes and showed a strong red emission signal because of the lower pH in lysosomes.

Red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) and orange fluorescent CDs (O-CDs) were obtained for the first time in an acid-mediated synthesis coupled with a liquid–liquid extraction separation strategy, using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as the precursor and phosphoric acid as an acidity regulator. The average particle sizes of the R-CDs and O-CDs were calculated as 3.13 nm and 3.45 nm, and their QYs as 4.16 % and 60.8 %, respectively. R-CDs demonstrated satisfactory stability, water solubility, acid sensitivity, and other properties, and can thus be used as fluorescence platforms for pH detection and cell imaging. Owing to the dual-emission character and outstanding water sensitivity, O-CDs can be applied as efficient ratio fluorescence sensors for detection of water in various organic solvents, including ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF, and acetone. Finally, cytotoxicity and cell imaging results on the R-CDs confirmed that R-CDs can serve as promising imaging probes for cytoplasm especially lysosomes, further revealing the potential of R-CDs in the field of biological imaging.

Yongqing Tao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lijuan Yang: . Meng Tian: Data curation, Investigation. Dandan Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Luchun Wang: Software, Investigation. Junji Wang: Software, Investigation. Tian Quan: Software, Investigation. Shaochi Liu: Software, Investigation. Kailian Zhang: Software, Investigation. Zhining Xia: Supervision, Writing – original draft. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107901.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
